The creek changed color for about two days. Muddy brown, like something upstream churned up the bottom.
Tracks that size do not come from anything I recognize. Too wide for a wolf, too narrow for a bear.
The ridge shifted after the last heavy rain. Not much, but enough that the old trail does not line up with the pass anymore.
Fish stopped appearing in the lower pool. The upper stretch still has them, so something is blocking the run between the two.
The water level in the spring dropped a full hand's width in less than a week. No drought, no diversion. It just receded.
Bats left the eastern caves all at once. A whole colony, gone overnight. They usually roost there year-round.
There is a patch of ground near the old foundation where nothing grows. Bare soil in a circle, maybe ten paces across.
The hawks are nesting lower than usual this season. The falconer says that means the prey has moved down from the heights.
We found a cluster of mushrooms growing in a perfect ring. Not unusual by itself, but this one was a good twenty paces in diameter.
The rock face on the north side is weeping moisture where it never did before. Something underground must have shifted.
A landslide blocked the ravine after the tremor. Small one, but enough to dam the stream and create a new pool.
The wildflowers bloomed a full month early. Everything is out of sequence and the pollinators have not caught up.
An old growth stump near the ruins is sprouting new shoots. Whatever killed the original must have finally leached out of the soil.
The ground near the hot spring is warm enough to feel through boot leather. It was not like that last year.
Deer have been coming closer to the walls. Either they are being pushed out of their usual range or they have lost their fear of us.
The moss on the standing stones grows on the wrong side. It should face the moisture but it faces the ridge instead.
There is a sinkhole forming in the meadow south of here. Slow, but the edges are crumbling inward a little more each week.
